speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think the accomplishments of the Jews would have been what they have been because what-- what-- if you look at the-- at the entire history of their accomplishments, of their contribution to the culture and to knowledge of the world-- when I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second to just read the slate. [INAUDIBLE] Continuing our interview with Dr. Herbert Zipper talking to Sandy Jacobson in Los Angeles, California. We're speaking English. It's the 9th of February, 1995. And this is tape two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to say, you have to be twice as good as everybody else because you must never forget that you are Jewish. Jewish meant, of course, the tradition, the tradition of the Jewish society, which is of course age old. They ask me, what is it? What is it really? Well, it's very difficult to put your finger on one thing. But the outstanding of the Jewish tradition is the utter respect for knowledge, the utter respect for the written word, the respect for books, the respect for the accomplishment of the mind. This is the guiding, and let's say, the innate thing of the Jewish tradition, regardless whether this has anything to do with religion or not. In our case, it was not religion at all. Religion was never-- never discussed at all. I was allowed to stop religious lessons when I was 12. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was actually the majority of the middle class in Vienna. The middle class in Vienna was agnostic.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had religious people in your family. Both of your grandfathers had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. You see, that was just about 50 years earlier. My grandfather was a rabbi. My other grandfather was a cantor, my father's father, who he never knew, because he died when he was 60-- 60 years old. I did never know him. My grandmother used to-- still keeps Jewish holidays in a certain way. But I think she more or less declared what was really necessary to abide by the Jewish rules. She was making the rules in herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you were growing up in Vienna--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this very tumultuous time, no food. You continued your education? You continued to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Of course I continued my education. I-- I graduated when I was 20-- when I was 17 in 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the gymnasium, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to the Goethe?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Goethe Gymnasium and then went straight-- I started already in the last year to-- went to the academy, to-- to go to my preliminary things going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which academy was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The State Academy of Music and-- and Performing Arts.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were some of your teachers there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was still [? Mandichevsky ?]. The other day, I just heard about him. He was-- he was a-- he was a very close friend of Brahms and actually did start collected-- the edition of the collected works of Brahms. But it's-- that would lead me too far away to tell you about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in a workshop with-- with Ravel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No with--

speaker: interviewer
text: With Strauss, with Richard Strauss.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a workshop. Strauss and later on for one semester Ravel were what they would call lecturers who come in-- come in once a month and discuss. No, I was a student really of Joseph Marx.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you see your profession in music? Were you-- were you planning to be a concert pianist, a performer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I tell you [INAUDIBLE]. One of the-- in the second year, I think, at the examination, Strauss was there and asked me, what-- what do you want to be? And I said, I want to be a pianist. He said, come on, a pianist. They're a dime a dozen. He said, you're pretty tall. He said, you should be a conductor. So you see, if a man like Richard Strauss, who for us was god, or-- or any teacher of-- in whom we had trust would say anything, we would do this exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why I started in the same year-- I think he managed it or he said it to someone. In one of the Viennese theaters, I became an assistant during my studies. You became--

speaker: interviewee
text: An assistant.

speaker: interviewer
text: To?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- in the-- in the theater, where I conducted Saturday afternoon performances for the kids. And there I started learning the trade. And he thought-- I think Strauss didn't think very much of our school of conducting. But that was an internal thing, and that was-- I wondered why he didn't-- didn't show me in. And I know now of course why, because it was really a mediocrity.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- I'm sorry?

speaker: interviewee
text: A mediocrity at that time, that the teacher at the conducting school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was-- that was very simple. I mean, from then on, the career of a successful student of the academy was-- was caved out. We knew exactly-- I had already-- my first contract was in-- in the last year of my study before my graduation. I had already a contract.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with German theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So besides the conducting, you also did a lot of music arranging, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did all kinds of musical things. First of all, I composed. My major was in composition. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell the story about how you had-- for a Hungarian friend, you were busy doing what you pretty sure were somebody else's compositions. He would call you, and he'd say, OK, we need something [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Orgun Sandor. He-- I met him here. Orgun Sandor. You see, we of course used our skills to earn some money because things were very different after the war than they were before the war. And Orgun Sandor always being a very clever Hungarian, a very gifted man, did accept too much work, which always had to be delivered in time-- in a certain time. And so he needed some-- needed some subcontractors, they call it. And I was his subcontractor for quite some time. I don't recall for how long. But I know for quite some time. And it's-- I did all kinds of scoring, especially for the-- for the Viennese operettas. I never knew who was the-- the composer. I began to-- from the style to think this was this one or this one. But we never asked questions. We never-- and-- and we were paid by the page, and that was it. So I did a lot of work there besides to earn some money. And Orgun Sandor then started also teaching in the conservatory in Budapest. It was one week in Vienna, two weeks in Budapest. And he must have had a student who couldn't-- I don't know why. Who couldn't write anything well. And so in many ways, he-- I started composing for somebody whom I didn't know. That was a bit better paid. And so I composed all kinds of things. He would call me in the morning. Well, usually, it was Tuesday morning. He called me up and said, Herbert, I need a string quartet, last movement, rondo form, 6/8. And he told me all the-- what the-- the prescription-- and not later than Saturday, 4 o'clock. So I sat down and wrote a nice, moving string quartet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were also write-- writing for cabarets, weren't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was way afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you are in your 20s, so when we're in the years-- in the 1920s. And you meet a beautiful, young woman. Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was already at-- at-- towards the end of the '20s. There were the '20s in Vienna. I mean, to explain what the '20s were-- it's very difficult because this was a real revolutionary time. I grew up at that time. I mean, I matured, in what we call today-- we call it-- of course, we called it at that time Red Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: See, Austria was a peculiar country. Austrian Vienna was vast majority Social Democrat. That was my-- the hinterland of Austria was mostly what is called Christian Social. That means the dominant force there was the Catholic Church. In Vienna, it was not the Catholic Church. In Vienna, it was the-- the city hall. And the city hall was meant by some very, very exciting people, especially two what we call today [GERMAN]. It means-- what would they be here? Like states rights kind of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. [GERMAN]. It means-- it's a position, the title, title of the people. There were two university professors, Tandler and Breitner, who revamped Vienna. First of all, Vienna became one of the cleanest cities in the world, while it was in 1919, it was one of the dirtiest. There-- there was no-- all the things didn't work. But what these two men tried to do is to recast the culture, the spirit, the attitude of the working class. It was a pipe dream, but it was a wonderful pipe dream. It was something that in the end didn't work. But the idea-- and now they begin to unwind the past and to try to uncover what happened at that time. And Red Vienna-- now they are writing books about it. Here I have seen quite a lot of publishing-- publications of Red Vienna. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Their dream was that the whole bourgeois culture-- that means the great mainstream of classical music, the great mainstream of classical literature, of the theater, of whatever there is-- of-- has to be brought to the working classes and raise the whole thing. This is, of course, the wrong way. There had been at that time really big-- big fads, really. For they-- they gave symphony concerts for the-- for the working class. But where they gave it was in the same places where the-- where the traditional concert houses were. People didn't go there. So the audience that they played for were exactly the same bourgeois audiences. I had at that time a very different idea. Breitner and Tandler built in every district of Vienna-- at that time, there were 21 districts-- a-- a working home. That means where the workers could learn all kinds of things, where they can listen to things, where they can have their own movies, for instance, and also their own concerts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I made a working agreement with the union and started playing concerts in every one of these, usually on Sundays, in every one of these things. And they were completely full. That's where the people go. They were-- they could come in slippers if they wanted to. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was-- this was the exciting '20s. And-- and I must say, Vienna became a different kind of city. And first of all, what they did architecturally for the-- for the workers, the workers' homes, which were entirely different from what they were before. They recorded where delegations from all over the world were coming to see what-- what they are-- what they do. You see, they-- they built in the short time of five or six years 64,000 apartments. That's not-- not hay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where did they have the money for this after the terrible deprivation of the earlier part of the decade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did they have the money? Taxes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things were getting back to  business? Life was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course, of course. Back to business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, yeah but then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't forget. When you cut off politically a huge country, the strings and the avenues to the capital are being kept open. The people who want to get the finer things, they still came to Vienna and bought things. The tourism did still work quite well, because Austria is a beautiful country, where it's most-- most beautiful to spend the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Zipper family prosper again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now that was-- that was the '20s. But then there was the depression. So then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the depression was of course universal. But it was before the depression, in 1927, when I met Trudl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to say it simple, I went-- my-- my sister was-- was still kept the young lady of-- of-- of the class in which she would not go out alone. She would have-- would have to be chaperoned. And I was one of the chaperones. And she decided she wanted to go to that very famous ball, Kunst Haus, where they-- they-- big exhibitions are in Vienna. There's always a ball in February. And she decided with two of her friends, young-- young girls-- she goes and she asks me to-- to be her chaperone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 23. And-- and the day before, she got ill, my sister. So she says, Herbert, these two girls, they already made their costumes. Would you go with them? Because they wouldn't be allowed either to go. So I went with them, took a book with me because I wasn't dancing. I wasn't doing anything like that. And I went in there and sat down in the corner and told the girls, go and have fun. And every 30 minutes, you come here and say you're all right just that I know what you're doing. And it was about half an hour that I was sitting there looking at my book, and I saw three people coming in, two girls and one boy, who had the fantastic costume. And they were not costumed but seemingly came from somewhere where they were dressed up. And I saw this little girl just arranging her hair. And I saw those hands doing-- I was electrified by the-- by the movement and by the hair and by the way she moved. She was tiny. She was about 4-- 4'9'-- 4-- yeah, 4'9'. She didn't grow very much since then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were six foot one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's funny. But any-- anyway, I approached her immediately and asked her if she would dance with me. That was of course-- And she started dancing with me, and she started laughing at me. Did you ever dance? No, you are the first one to tell me how to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we practically danced-- danced all night. Then I stayed-- the other woman was her sister. So she was in with her sister and the sister's boyfriend. So they were all under-- under control, so to say. And when I-- when I went with the kids home, I got her address and telephone number of course, and sent her a bundle of roses. And that she never got. She was only 14. Nobody said. Nobody [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And she thought that I'm an old man, you know, nine years older. So any-- anyone who was grown up would take it so seriously, sending her roses. That-- she told me later that she couldn't sleep about that, the idea that somebody would send her roses. And that started it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was to be the love of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was the-- that was the moment when I thought the relationship between-- between the sexes is something different than what I was used to. You see, you have no idea what this post-war life was. It's-- it's very difficult to explain it that much. It's also not necessary. But it was a revolution against these very tight, bourgeois ethics that were completely untrue. And if you read Arthur Schnitzler, you will know what-- what it was all about. And sadly, this was gone, that-- that overpowering throne of the Habsburgs was gone. The aristocracy was still there, but it didn't have-- have no say.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still an entirely different [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] spirit that-- that demonstrated itself by the women having short-- short skirts. You don't know that of course anymore. How could you? Skirts. The bubikopf, short. You know, but expressions you know. Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, of course, an expression of-- of the new time. And, therefore, we had to also learn a different way. And we were the ones also who taught the different way, my brother and I and our friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk, please, now about the '30s, after the depression and-- and the political climate.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was during the depression that I started-- the depression started in '29. And it was at that time that I got my first opera engagement in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was the time when radicalism became on every side [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a way of life. We became radical socialists. They became radical fascists. And it is not known today that the socialists were still in the plurality all over Europe. But they didn't have the support of the big business, the big industries, the big money sources that still were available. The fascists got the money. We got the voices. So in Germany, we realized immediately that Hitler never got more than-- less than a third of the votes, while the socialists and the Communists got twice as many. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the leadership was very stupid. They never would get together in Germany. However, in the fall of 1930-- in November of 1932, when I was in Dusseldorf-- a different job already-- it looked as if the people would rise up to the fascists' rankings. I mean, anyone with any senses would have listened at that time to Hitler's speeches, as I did--

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually attended one of his speeches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I attended some of his meetings, some of his rallies in order to know, to learn what's happening because learning is a profession for me. And he lost-- there were two elections in this year, one in March, one in November, '32. And he lost in November more than two million votes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Two million votes in America is nothing. But don't forget there are not that many voters. I mean, there was about one fourth of the voters that America has today. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that was-- so everybody became relaxed. They said, well, the danger is over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought so too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did? When you had gone to the meetings, the-- the-- where Hitler spoke, did you feel a chill? Did you-- what-- what were your feelings as you sat there in these meetings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can tell you. It's very difficult to say it in-- in a few words. But it's very difficult to manipulate me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not easily manipulated by words, by anything. I can be manipulated by a great piece of music, by a great sculpture. I mean, [INAUDIBLE] always get me something. By the way, I didn't tell you that I also at the same time did a lot of sculpting. But that's beside the point. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, I was completely immune, I mean, as-- as immune as one possibly could be.

speaker: interviewer
text: But as a Jew, did you sit there and think to yourself, this looks very bad for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I thought it-- it is a disaster. I mean, not just bad, it was almost disastrous because what he did was to blame every possible ills of the German situation at that time, the economic situation, the social situation, everything that-- that didn't work on the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this always has happened. It was unchecked. It has always happened. He knew exactly what-- what he was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then he lost the election, and you felt OK. And that is over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I said, well, the people-- they got their mind straightened out a little bit, and he lost. But then of course the German government didn't-- didn't-- anyway work itself out of that disaster. The Communists and the Social Democrats didn't want to join forces, which they could have done very easily. So Hindenburg, that old man, was persuaded to try it with Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dusseldorf.
